title: Pulp Invasion! - An OK Game with a Great Look
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aD-WkrzMJrk

Kicking off a month of science-fiction coverage, Daniel (@dungeondive) examines Pulp Invasion, a small-box bag-building resource-management game by Todd Sanders (Thrilling Tales of Adventure), picked up on a viewer's recommendation along with its X1 expansion. You play a rim captain secretly working for the intergalactic council, visiting infiltrated planets to find world-shattering super-science weapons before three alien factions can invade. Pick a captain with combat, science and diplomacy stats; draw a couple of ability dice; then push your luck pulling cubes from a bag to overcome colour-coded encounters, replenish stats, and search for the hidden weapons that win the game.

Daniel is clear-eyed: this is a mechanisms-forward game with a pasted-on theme — change the setting and it would still work — and he gets "somewhat" rather than a lot of joy from it. What keeps it on the shelf is the look. The art is genuine vintage pulp licensed from Steeger Properties (he spots likely Virgil Finlay pieces), and the game looks terrific set up. That sparks a tangent on copyright and old pulp art that should arguably be public domain, plus a loving detour through his own shelf of classic SF paperbacks. A fair, art-first appraisal of a game he likes more as an object than an experience.

----

Daniel admits he bought Pulp Invasion mostly because it looks gorgeous on the table, not for the mechanisms. Have you ever kept (or bought) a game purely for its art, even knowing the gameplay underneath was just okay?
